High-Level Design: Real-time Restaurant Order Metrics for a Food Delivery Platform
HARD
role
Software Development Engineer 3
Round 3
High-Level Design
System Design (HLD)
exp
#488 · Sept 2025
● offer
High-Level Design (HLD)
System Design
real-time analytics
data pipelines
Streaming Data
Database Design
Monitoring
Aggregation

▸
View problem statement
Problem statement
Design a system for a food delivery platform (like Uber Eats) that provides restaurant owners with real-time aggregated metrics about their orders. The system should process a continuous stream of new order data and make the following metrics available on a dashboard:

Total Value of Orders: For each restaurant, the sum of all order values within the last 1 hour, last 1 day, and last 1 week.
Top K Food Items: For each restaurant, a list of the top K most ordered food items (by quantity) within the last 1 hour, last 1 day, and last 1 week.
The metrics should be as real-time as possible, reflecting changes almost immediately after an order is placed (e.g., sub-minute refresh rate for dashboards).

Consider aspects such as data ingestion, real-time processing, storage solutions for aggregated and raw data, and efficient retrieval for dashboards used by thousands of restaurant owners.

Requirements:
Data Ingestion: Handle a high volume of incoming order data from various restaurants (millions of orders per day).
Real-time Processing: Aggregate data in near real-time to update metrics for different time windows.
Historical Data: Store and manage historical data efficiently for these time-windowed aggregations.
Querying: Efficiently serve queries for aggregated metrics for individual restaurants, catering to thousands of concurrent users.
Scalability: The system must be able to scale horizontally to accommodate growth in orders, restaurants, and active users.
Reliability & Accuracy: Ensure data accuracy, consistency, and high availability of the metrics system.

## 1. Requirements (Functional + Non-Functional)

> 🎙️ "Here's how I'll structure this: I'll lock the requirements and rough scale, sketch the API and data model, draw the high-level design one metric at a time, then go deep on the two or three hardest parts — the windowed aggregation and the top-K — checking in with you as I go. Sound good?"

The core tension: we need **near-real-time** (sub-minute) metrics over **sliding time windows** (1h / 1d / 1w) for **millions of orders/day**, served to **thousands of concurrent dashboards** — so the whole game is pre-aggregating on the write path so reads are cheap, while keeping windows accurate as they slide.

🏢 I'll target a **Google/Meta-style bar** — real distributed-streaming depth — but this problem smells like Uber/DoorDash, so I'll keep real-time and hot-restaurant skew front-of-mind. Tell me if it's Amazon and I'll make cost/ops lead.

**Functional (above the line):**
- **FR1 — Total order value per restaurant** over last 1h, 1d, 1w (rolling).
- **FR2 — Top-K food items per restaurant** by quantity over last 1h, 1d, 1w.
- **FR3 — Serve these to a dashboard** with sub-minute freshness for thousands of concurrent owners.

**Below the line (out of scope):** order placement/payments itself, delivery tracking, ML demand forecasting, owner auth UI, arbitrary ad-hoc OLAP (we'll mention a cold path for it).

**Non-Functional:**
- **Freshness:** metric reflects an order within **< 60s** (p99 end-to-end ingest→visible).
- **Read latency:** dashboard query **p99 < 200ms**.
- **Availability:** **99.9%** for the read/dashboard path (metrics are important but not payment-critical); ingestion **99.99%** (never drop orders).
- **Accuracy:** totals within **~1%** of exact in steady state, **eventually exact**; counts must not double-count on retries (idempotent).
- **Scale:** millions of orders/day, horizontally scalable on orders, restaurants, and viewers.
- **Consistency:** metrics are **eventually consistent** with a bounded staleness of one window-slice (~60s); the *owner's own* recent orders should appear within that bound (read-your-own-writes within 60s).

> ⚠️ Gaps I'd flag out loud: a true *sliding* 1-week window that's exact-to-the-second is expensive; I'll approximate it with fixed buckets (minute/hour granularity) and be explicit about the error bound rather than pretend it's exact.

> 💬 "The whole problem is reads vastly outnumber meaningful writes and the windows slide, so I'll pre-aggregate into time buckets on ingest and make the dashboard a cheap lookup."

🎙️ **Script:** "So we're building owner-facing analytics: total order value and top-K items, each over a rolling hour, day, and week, refreshing in under a minute. We take millions of orders a day in and fan them out to thousands of dashboards, so it's read-heavy on serving but the real work is keeping sliding-window aggregates fresh and accurate. I'll treat ingestion as can't-drop-data, and the metrics themselves as eventually consistent within about a minute — and I'll be upfront that a to-the-second weekly window is something I'll approximate with buckets."

## 2. Clarifying Questions & Assumptions

Questions I'd ask, and why each changes the design:
- **"How many orders/day and peak multiplier?"** — sets stream partition count and whether a single aggregator node can hold hot-restaurant state. *Assume ~10M/day, 5x peak.*
- **"How many restaurants and how skewed?"** — top-K state is per-restaurant; a few mega-chains create hot keys. *Assume ~500K restaurants, heavy skew (Zipfian).*
- **"How big is K?"** — K≈10–100 changes whether exact counts or a sketch is fine. *Assume K ≤ 20.*
- **"Exact or approximate weekly numbers acceptable?"** — decides exact bucket-sum vs. sketches (Count-Min/SpaceSaving). *Assume ~1% error OK, eventually exact.*
- **"Can orders be cancelled/modified after placement?"** — needs negative deltas / retractions, not just additive counts. *Assume yes — cancellations happen; design for reversible aggregates.*
- **"Order value currency/timezone for windows?"** — window boundaries and currency normalization. *Assume UTC windows, value pre-normalized to USD cents.*
- **"Is late/out-of-order event data common?"** — drives watermarks and allowed-lateness. *Assume seconds-to-minutes lateness possible (mobile clients).*

🤝 Checkpoint: "Those are my assumptions — 10M orders/day, 500K skewed restaurants, K≤20, ~1% accuracy with eventual exactness, and cancellations exist. Want to adjust any before I size it?"

Let me now compute the few numbers that actually drive decisions.

orders_day = 10_000_000
sec = 86400
avg_qps = orders_day/sec
peak = avg_qps*5
print("avg orders/s", round(avg_qps), "peak", round(peak))

# read side
restaurants = 500_000
# assume 10% owners watching during peak, dashboard polls every 30s
active = 0.10*restaurants
read_qps = active/30
print("active viewers", int(active), "read qps", round(read_qps))

# storage raw orders
bytes_per_order = 300  # id, restid, items, value, ts
raw_day = orders_day*bytes_per_order
print("raw/day GB", round(raw_day/1e9,2), "raw/week GB", round(raw_day*7/1e9,1))

# pre-aggregated buckets: per restaurant per minute bucket
# value agg: restid+minute+sum  ~ 40 bytes; items topk: store per-item counts per minute
# minutes in a week
mins_week = 7*24*60
# value buckets per restaurant for a week (1-min granularity)
val_rows = restaurants*mins_week
print("value bucket rows (1wk,1min)", f"{val_rows/1e9:.1f}B", "bytes", round(val_rows*40/1e9,1),"GB")

# items: assume avg 30 distinct items per restaurant, count per minute is sparse;
# better hourly granularity for week. recompute hourly
hours_week=7*24

print("item hourly rows/wk", f"{item_rows/1e6:.0f}M", "GB", round(item_rows*30/1e9,1))

# redis hot state: top-k per restaurant per window kept in memory
# per restaurant: 3 windows * (1 value + ~30 item counters) ~ small
per_rest_bytes = 3*(8 + 30*16)
print("redis MB for all restaurants", round(restaurants*per_rest_bytes/1e6))

content.text:
## 3. Scale & Capacity (talkable numbers) I'm deferring full estimation and only computing what forces a decision. | Metric | Number | Say-it-out-loud | |---|---|---| | Avg ingest | 116 orders/s | "~100/s average" | | Peak ingest | ~580 orders/s | "~600/s at peak" | | Dashboard reads (10% owners, 30s poll) | ~1,700 QPS | "under 2K reads/s" | | Raw orders storage | ~3 GB/day, ~21 GB/week | "tiny — gigabytes" | | Per-minute value buckets (1wk) | ~5B rows, ~200GB | "big if I go 1-min for a week" | | Hot per-restaurant state in Redis | ~730 MB | "fits in one Redis node's RAM" | **The one number that forces the design:** ingest is only **~600 writes/s at peak** — trivially small — but reads are **~1,700 QPS** and the *aggregate-maintenance* cost is what hurts. The flip point: if I keep **1-minute buckets for a full week per restaurant** I get ~5B rows / ~200GB and huge fan-out; that's the threshold where I switch to **mixed granularity** (1-min buckets only for the hour window, roll up to hourly buckets for day/week). That single decision cuts storage ~20x. 🧠 **QPS** *(queries per second — how many requests/sec the tier must serve)*. **TTL** *(time-to-live — auto-expiry on a cache entry so stale data self-cleans)*. 🎙️ **Script:** "The surprise here is that writes are tiny — about 600 orders a second at peak — so ingestion isn't the bottleneck at all. The pressure is on keeping sliding-window aggregates fresh and serving roughly 1,700 dashboard reads a second. The trap is storage on the aggregates: if I naively keep one-minute buckets for a whole week per restaurant that's five billion rows. So I'll keep fine one-minute buckets only for the hour view and roll up to hourly buckets for the day and week — that keeps it small and fast." ## 4. Core Entities - **Order** — a placed order: id, restaurant, list of (item, qty, price), total value, event timestamp. - **Restaurant** — the owner-facing tenant; everything is partitioned by restaurant id. - **Food Item** — a menu item within a restaurant; the unit we rank for top-K. - **Time-Bucket Aggregate** — pre-computed sum / item-counts for one (restaurant, granularity, bucket) slice. This is the heart of the system. - **Metric Snapshot** — the materialized answer for one (restaurant, window) the dashboard reads. This is a first draft of the nouns — I'll nail the exact fields in the data model after the high-level design. 🎙️ **Script:** "The key nouns are Orders coming in, Restaurants as the tenant we partition everything by, Food Items as what we rank, and then the thing that actually makes this fast — pre-computed time-bucket aggregates that the dashboard just reads as a snapshot. I'll firm up fields later." ## 5. API / Interface Default REST. Ingestion is internal (from the order service via a stream), dashboards read. **FR1/FR2 reads — one endpoint, window as param:** ``` GET /v1/restaurants/{id}/metrics?window=1h|1d|1w Auth: owner JWT; {id} must match token's restaurant claim 200 -> { window: "1h", total_value_cents: 1234500, top_k_items: [{item_id, name, qty}, ...], as_of: "2025-09-01T12:34:00Z" // freshness stamp } ``` Why GET: pure read, cacheable, CDN/edge-friendly. `as_of` lets the UI show freshness and lets us detect staleness. **Ingestion (internal, not public REST) — events onto the log:** ``` OrderPlaced { order_id, restaurant_id, items:[{item_id, qty, unit_price_cents}], total_cents, ts } OrderCancelled { order_id, restaurant_id, ts } // emits negative deltas ``` Each event carries `order_id` as the **idempotency/natural key** so retries dedup. **Security:** caller identity comes from the **JWT** *(signed token proving who's logged in)* — never trust a client-supplied restaurant id, timestamp, or value. An owner can only read their own restaurant. > 💬 "One read endpoint parameterized by window keeps the API tiny; ingestion isn't a public API, it's events the order service already emits." 🎙️ **Script:** "On the read side it's literally one GET per restaurant with the window as a query param, returning the total, the top-K list, and an as-of timestamp so the UI can show how fresh it is. Writes aren't a public API — the order service emits OrderPlaced and OrderCancelled events keyed by order id, and that order id is my idempotency key so a retried event never double-counts." ## 6. High-Level Design (built one requirement at a time) ### 6.1 "Total order value per restaurant over 1h / 1d / 1w" Start minimal: an order event arrives, we add its value into time buckets for that restaurant, and the dashboard reads the sum of the relevant buckets. New components: an **event log** (Kafka), a **stream processor** (Flink), a **bucket store**, and a serving cache. 🧠 **Kafka** *(a durable, replayable log that buffers a firehose of events so nothing is lost if a consumer is slow — here it holds order events between the order service and our processors)*. 🧠 **Flink** *(a stream-processing engine that maintains running aggregates over time windows with exactly-once guarantees — it does the per-restaurant summing)*. ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph Clients OS[Order service] DB1[Owner dashboard] end subgraph Ingest K[[Kafka order log
partition by restaurant]] end subgraph Stream F[Flink aggregator
value sums] end subgraph Serving AGG[(Bucket store
restaurant minute sum)] RD[(Redis
window snapshots)] API[Metrics API] end OS -->|1 OrderPlaced| K K -->|2 consume| F F -->|3 upsert bucket delta| AGG F -->|4 update window snapshot| RD DB1 -->|5 GET metrics| API API -->|6 read snapshot| RD API -.->|6b miss recompute| AGG classDef hot fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3; classDef cold fill:#3d1f0b,stroke:#d29922,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3; class RD,API,DB1 hot; class K,F,AGG,OS cold; ``` Flow: **(1)** order service publishes OrderPlaced to Kafka partitioned by restaurant_id (so all of one restaurant's events land on one partition → one consumer → no cross-node locking). **(2)** Flink consumes. **(3)** It upserts the order value into the current **minute bucket** `(restaurant, minute) += value` (state change: bucket sum increases). **(4)** It refreshes the three window snapshots (1h = sum of last 60 minute-buckets, etc.) in Redis. **(5)** Dashboard GETs. **(6)** API returns the Redis snapshot; on miss it recomputes from the bucket store. > 💬 "Partition Kafka by restaurant so each restaurant's stream is owned by exactly one processor — that removes locking and makes the aggregate trivially correct per key." ### 6.2 "Top-K food items per restaurant over each window" Same pipeline, second aggregate. For each item in the order, Flink increments a per-restaurant, per-bucket **item-count** map; the top-K is the highest counts across the window's buckets. New component: an **item-count state** in Flink + a top-K materializer. For the hot path we keep a small **heap/sorted-set** per (restaurant, window) in Redis (a Redis Sorted Set gives ordered top-K natively). 🧠 **Redis Sorted Set** *(an in-memory structure that keeps members ranked by score — here item → quantity — so reading top-K is an O(log n) range read, no scan)*. ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph Clients OS[Order service] DB1[Owner dashboard] end K[[Kafka order log]] subgraph Stream F[Flink aggregator
value + item counts] end subgraph Serving AGG[(Bucket store
value + item counts
per minute/hour)] RD[(Redis
value snapshots +
topK sorted sets)] API[Metrics API] end OS -->|1 OrderPlaced| K K -->|2| F F -->|3 value + item deltas| AGG F -->|4 update snapshot + ZSET| RD DB1 -->|5 GET| API API -->|6 read snapshot + ZRANGE| RD classDef hot fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3; classDef cold fill:#3d1f0b,stroke:#d29922,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3; class RD,API,DB1 hot; class K,F,AGG,OS cold; ``` Flow adds: **(3)** Flink also writes per-item count deltas; **(4)** updates the per-window Sorted Set in Redis (`ZINCRBY restaurant:1h item_42 qty`); **(6)** dashboard reads top-K via `ZREVRANGE … 0 K`. > 💬 "Top-K is just a ranked set per restaurant per window — Redis sorted sets give me ordered top-K for free, so the read is a single range query." ### 6.3 "Serve thousands of dashboards with sub-minute freshness" The serving layer is **read-from-Redis** behind a stateless API tier, fronted by a load balancer. Snapshots are refreshed by Flink every ~10–30s, so a poll-every-30s dashboard always sees < 60s-old data. We add a short **CDN/edge TTL (5–10s)** on the GET to absorb refresh-spam from many tabs. 🧠 **CDN** *(edge cache near users — here it collapses many identical dashboard polls into one origin hit for a few seconds)*. **Final (high-level):** ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph Clients OS[Order service] DB1[Dashboards x1000s] end CDN[CDN / edge cache
5-10s TTL] LB[Load balancer] K[[Kafka
partition by restaurant]] subgraph Stream F[Flink aggregator
value sums + item counts
checkpointed state] end subgraph Serving RD[(Redis cluster
snapshots + topK ZSETs
key=restaurant:window)] API[Metrics API
stateless] end AGG[(Aggregate store - Cassandra
restaurant,granularity,bucket -> sum,counts)] RAW[(Raw orders - S3/warehouse
cold, replay + audit)] OS -->|1 events| K K -->|2| F F -->|3 rollup buckets| AGG F -->|4 snapshots| RD K -->|3b archive| RAW DB1 -->|5 GET| CDN --> LB --> API API -->|6 read| RD API -.->|6b miss| AGG classDef hot fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3; classDef cold fill:#3d1f0b,stroke:#d29922,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3; class CDN,LB,API,RD,DB1 hot; class K,F,AGG,RAW,OS cold; ``` Schema columns that carry weight: **Cassandra** keyed by `(restaurant_id, granularity)` partition, clustering on `bucket_ts` → enables a fast range-scan of "last 60 minute buckets". **Redis** key `restaurant:{id}:{window}` holds the precomputed value + the top-K ZSET. 🎙️ **Script:** "End to end: the order service drops events on Kafka partitioned by restaurant, so each restaurant is owned by one Flink task with no cross-node contention. Flink keeps running sums and item counts, writes durable rollup buckets to Cassandra, and pushes ready-to-serve snapshots and top-K sorted sets into Redis every ten to thirty seconds. The dashboard just does one GET that hits a short-lived CDN cache, then Redis — so a thousands-of-users read is a single in-memory lookup, and the data is always under a minute old. Raw events also get archived to a warehouse so I can replay and audit." 🤝 Checkpoint: "That's the skeleton end to end. The two hardest parts are the sliding-window aggregation as windows expire and the top-K under hot-restaurant skew — which do you want me to go deep on first?" ## 7. Data Model & Storage | Entity | Key fields | Store | Why | |---|---|---|---| | Raw order event | order_id (PK), restaurant_id, items[], total_cents, ts | **Kafka → S3/warehouse** | Append-only firehose; cheap, replayable, audit/recompute source of truth | | Value bucket | (restaurant_id, granularity) part, bucket_ts clust, sum_cents, order_count | **Cassandra** | Write-heavy upserts + range-scan a window; partition by restaurant scales horizontally | | Item-count bucket | (restaurant_id, granularity, bucket_ts) part, item_id clust, qty | **Cassandra** | Same access pattern; range-scan items in a bucket | | Window snapshot | restaurant:{id}:{window} → total + topK ZSET | **Redis cluster** | Sub-ms reads for the dashboard hot path; TTL self-heals | 🧠 **Cassandra** *(a horizontally-partitioned NoSQL store optimized for high write throughput and key-range reads via an LSM-tree — here it holds the durable time buckets, sharded by restaurant)*. **LSM-tree** *(write-optimized structure that batches writes in memory then flushes sorted files — great for our upsert-heavy buckets)*. **Consistency per op:** bucket upserts are **idempotent by order_id** (we store processed order_ids with TTL to dedup replays); snapshot reads are **eventually consistent** (≤ window-slice stale). Cancellations write **negative deltas** into the current bucket so totals self-correct. **Granularity decision (from §3 math):** minute buckets for the 1h window; hourly buckets for 1d/1w. This caps the week scan at 168 hourly buckets instead of 10,080 minute buckets. 🎙️ **Script:** "Raw events live cheaply in Kafka and a warehouse as the source of truth. The durable aggregates go in Cassandra, partitioned by restaurant and clustered by bucket time, because the access pattern is exactly 'upsert this bucket, then range-scan the last N buckets.' The dashboard never touches Cassandra on the happy path — it reads Redis. And I keep minute buckets only for the hour, hourly for the day and week, which is what keeps storage sane." ## 8. Deep Dives — Bad → Good → Great 🆘 If you get stuck: go back to "writes are tiny, reads and window-maintenance are the cost," and reason forward from the bucket model. ### How do we compute sliding 1h/1d/1w windows in real time? **#### Bad: recompute from raw orders on each dashboard read.** Approach: on GET, scan all of a restaurant's orders in the window and sum. Challenges: a popular restaurant has thousands of orders/week; 1,700 read QPS each scanning thousands of rows melts the DB. ⚠️ **Trap:** doing aggregation at read time — it can't hit p99 200ms and doesn't scale with viewers. **#### Good: tumbling-window aggregation in Flink, one row per minute.** Approach: Flink maintains a per-(restaurant, minute) tumbling window; on read, sum the last 60 minutes / 24 hours / 7 days of buckets. Challenges: a *strict* sliding window needs the oldest slice to drop continuously; summing 10,080 minute-buckets for a week per read is wasteful, and storage explodes (the 5B-row problem). **#### Great: hierarchical buckets + precomputed rolling snapshot.** Approach: keep **minute buckets** (for 1h) and **hourly rollups** (for 1d/1w). Flink keeps a *running snapshot* per window and updates it incrementally: each minute it **adds the new slice and subtracts the expired slice** — this is the sliding window done as O(1) deltas, not a re-scan. Snapshots live in Redis; Cassandra holds buckets for recovery/recompute. ```mermaid flowchart LR E[Order event] --> M[Minute bucket +value] M --> H[Hourly rollup] M -->|add new slice| S1[1h snapshot] S1 -->|subtract expired minute| S1 H -->|add/subtract hour| S2[1d snapshot] H --> S3[1w snapshot] S1 --> RD[(Redis)] S2 --> RD S3 --> RD classDef hot fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3; class S1,S2,S3,RD hot; ``` Math: week window = 168 hourly buckets, not 10,080 minute buckets → ~60x less work and storage. Why real systems do this: incremental add/subtract on fixed buckets gives true rolling windows cheaply, and the bucket store lets us **replay** to fix errors. 🎙️ **Script:** "I keep minute buckets for the hour view and roll up to hourly buckets for day and week. The trick for sliding windows is that I don't re-sum on read — Flink keeps a running snapshot and each minute it adds the new slice and subtracts the one that just expired, so it's constant work. Redis holds the ready snapshot, Cassandra holds the buckets so I can always recompute." 🧠 If they ask "why not exact-to-the-second windows?": "Exact second-granularity for a week is 600K slices per restaurant — the storage and per-read cost don't justify it. Minute granularity bounds error to under one minute of orders, well within my ~1% accuracy SLA." ### How do we keep Top-K under hot-restaurant skew? **#### Good: exact per-item counters + Redis Sorted Set per window.** Approach: `ZINCRBY restaurant:{id}:{window} qty item` on each order; read `ZREVRANGE 0 K`. For a typical restaurant with ~30–200 distinct items this is exact and tiny. Challenges: a mega-chain promotion can make one ZSET huge and one Redis shard hot; and item counts also need the expired-slice subtraction. **#### Great: bucketed item counts + SpaceSaving sketch for huge tenants.** Approach: For normal restaurants, exact bucketed counts (add new minute, subtract expired). For the rare **giant tenant**, use a **SpaceSaving / Count-Min sketch** *(a fixed-memory structure that tracks approximate top items without storing every key)* capped at a few hundred counters — top-K of K≤20 stays accurate even if the long tail is approximate. Shard hot restaurants across Redis with a per-restaurant key so one mega-tenant doesn't collocate with others. 🧠 **Count-Min Sketch** *(a compact array of counters hashed multiple ways that estimates item frequencies in fixed memory — used so a viral item's restaurant doesn't blow memory)*. ⚠️ **Trap:** assuming uniform restaurants — a celebrity-chain promotion is the hot-key problem here. Relief: dedicated shard + sketch; new ceiling is bounded memory regardless of cardinality. 🎙️ **Script:** "For top-K I lean on Redis sorted sets keyed per restaurant per window, so reading the top items is one ranged query. Most restaurants have a small menu so exact counts are cheap. For a rare mega-chain that could blow up one shard, I switch that tenant to a bounded SpaceSaving sketch — top-20 stays accurate while the long tail is approximate — and I give hot tenants their own shard so they don't poison neighbors." 🧠 If they ask "won't the sketch be inaccurate?": "Only for the long tail; SpaceSaving guarantees the true top items aren't missed when their frequency exceeds the error bound, which for K≤20 on a high-volume tenant always holds." ### How do we guarantee accuracy with retries, cancellations, and late events? **#### Great: idempotent processing + exactly-once Flink + retractions + watermarks.** Approach: Each event has `order_id`; Flink uses **checkpointing + idempotent Cassandra upserts keyed by order_id** so a replay after failure doesn't double-count (**exactly-once** effect). Cancellations emit **negative deltas** keyed to the original order so totals self-correct. **Watermarks + allowed-lateness** *(a moving "we've seen everything up to time T" marker)* let late mobile events land in the right bucket within a grace window; events later than the grace go to a correction job that recomputes from raw S3 nightly → **eventually exact**. 🎙️ **Script:** "Accuracy rests on three things: every event is keyed by order id so retries are idempotent and Flink's checkpointing gives me effectively exactly-once, cancellations are just negative deltas into the bucket, and watermarks let slightly-late orders still land correctly. Anything later than the grace window gets fixed by a nightly recompute from the raw archive, so the numbers converge to exact." 🧠 If they ask "exactly-once is hard — really?": "End-to-end exactly-once needs idempotent sinks, which I have via order_id upserts; Flink gives exactly-once on its state, the sink dedups, so the combination is effectively exactly-once without distributed transactions." ## 9. Reliability, Failure Modes & Cost **Availability:** read path **99.9%** (multi-AZ Redis + stateless API behind LB); ingestion **99.99%** (Kafka replication factor 3). Per-dependency degradation: - **Redis down:** API falls back to recompute from Cassandra buckets (slower, ~50ms) — degraded, not down. Serve `as_of` so UI shows staleness. - **Flink down:** Kafka buffers (retention 24h+); on recovery it replays from last checkpoint — no data loss, just freshness lag. Dashboards show last-known + staleness banner. - **Cassandra down:** snapshots still served from Redis; bucket writes queue in Flink state. - **Hot shard:** dedicated shard + sketch (§8). **RPO/RTO:** Raw events in Kafka/S3 → **RPO ~0** (replicated log is source of truth). Aggregates are derived → **RTO minutes** by replay. Snapshots are disposable. Restore is tested by periodic replay-from-S3 into a staging cluster. **Multi-AZ vs region:** multi-AZ default; multi-region active-passive for DR (Kafka mirror), failover triggered by region health-check breaching for >2 min. **Cost (rough monthly):** dominant driver is **Kafka + Flink compute and Redis RAM**, not storage (raw is only ~21GB/week). Ballpark: a small Flink cluster + a ~3-node Redis cluster + Cassandra ~3 nodes → low **tens of thousands/month**. Egress is small (JSON metrics). **Ops:** SLO burn-rate alerts on freshness (as_of age > 90s) and read p99; distributed tracing ingest→Redis; runbook for "freshness lag" = check Flink checkpoint/lag. 🎙️ **Script:** "Because the raw event log is the source of truth and everything downstream is derived, my recovery story is just replay — RPO near zero, RTO in minutes. If Redis dies I recompute from Cassandra; if Flink dies Kafka buffers and we replay from a checkpoint; the dashboard always shows an as-of timestamp so degraded freshness is visible, not silent. Cost is dominated by streaming compute and Redis memory, not storage, since the raw data is only gigabytes." ## 10. Trade-off Ledger - **Minute/hourly bucket granularity vs exact sliding windows** — gave up to-the-second precision for ~60x less storage/compute; reverses if a customer demands second-level accuracy or order volume per restaurant drops enough that minute buckets for a week become cheap. - **Eventual consistency (≤60s) vs strong** — gave up instant exactness for cheap, scalable reads; reverses if metrics ever drive money (payouts) and need transactional accuracy — then read-from-buckets with stronger guarantees. - **Exact counts vs sketches for top-K** — exact by default, sketch only for mega-tenants; reverses fully toward sketches if restaurant menu cardinality grows huge or memory becomes the binding constraint. 🎙️ **Script:** "The three calls I'd flag: bucket granularity over exact windows, eventual consistency over strong, and exact counts over sketches except for giants. All three trade a little precision for a lot of scale, and each flips back if accuracy becomes worth more than cost — for instance if these numbers ever drove restaurant payouts." ## 11. Likely Interviewer Questions & Answers **1. Why pre-aggregate instead of a real-time OLAP DB like Druid/Pinot?** Druid/Pinot would actually fit this well and I'd happily use a managed one. I chose explicit Flink+buckets to control the sliding-window semantics and the hot-key/sketch handling precisely, and because raw volume is small. The trade-off is more components to operate vs. a single query engine. > 💬 "Honestly Pinot is a strong alternative here; I went custom for fine control of windows and hot keys, but I'd evaluate Pinot to cut operational surface." **2. How exactly do you expire the oldest slice from a rolling window?** Each window snapshot is `sum of buckets in [now-W, now]`. A timer fires per minute/hour; it adds the newest bucket and subtracts the bucket that just fell out of range. Buckets persist in Cassandra so a missed timer is recoverable by recompute. > 💬 "It's add-the-new-slice, subtract-the-expired-slice — constant work, not a rescan." **3. A celebrity restaurant gets 100x traffic — what breaks and what do you do?** Its Kafka partition and Redis shard become hot. Kafka stays fine (600/s total is low), but its ZSET and Flink task key get heavy. I give that tenant a dedicated Redis shard and switch its top-K to a bounded sketch, and I can sub-partition its Kafka key by item-hash if one task saturates. > 💬 "Isolate the hot tenant onto its own shard and cap its memory with a sketch." **4. How do you not double-count when Flink replays after a crash?** Cassandra upserts are keyed by order_id within a bucket, and Flink checkpoints its offsets; on replay it re-applies the same idempotent writes, so the result is identical. Snapshots are recomputed from buckets, so they're self-correcting. > 💬 "Idempotent upserts keyed by order id plus checkpointed offsets give effectively exactly-once." **5. An order is cancelled an hour later — does the total fix itself?** Yes — OrderCancelled emits a negative delta into the current bucket referencing the original order_id, so the running snapshot decreases. The nightly recompute from raw guarantees it's eventually exact even if the cancel arrived after the original bucket expired. > 💬 "Cancellations are negative deltas; the nightly recompute backstops anything that arrived late." **6. How is read p99 < 200ms achieved for 1,700 QPS?** Every happy-path read is a single Redis lookup plus a ZRANGE — sub-millisecond — behind a stateless API and a 5–10s CDN cache that collapses duplicate polls. Cassandra is only touched on Redis miss. > 💬 "It's one in-memory read; the DB isn't in the hot path." **7. What if Kafka is down?** The order service buffers/retries to a local outbox; metrics freshness lags but no orders are lost since order placement is a separate durable system. We alert on consumer lag and freshness. > 💬 "Kafka outage degrades freshness, not correctness — events replay when it's back." **8. Why partition by restaurant_id and not order_id?** All aggregates are per-restaurant, so co-locating a restaurant's events on one partition/task removes cross-node coordination and makes the windowed state local. Downside is skew, handled by sub-partitioning hot tenants. > 💬 "Per-restaurant partitioning makes the aggregate state local and lock-free." **9. How do you handle late/out-of-order events?** Flink watermarks with an allowed-lateness grace (a few minutes) route slightly-late events into the correct event-time bucket. Beyond the grace, they're caught by the nightly recompute from S3. > 💬 "Watermarks for the common lateness, nightly recompute for the stragglers." **10. Storage for a week of per-minute buckets is huge — how do you bound it?** I only keep minute granularity for the hour window and roll up to hourly buckets for day/week, cutting the week from ~10,080 slices to 168 per restaurant. Old minute buckets TTL out after the hour window passes. > 💬 "Mixed granularity plus TTL on fine buckets keeps storage at gigabytes." **11. How would you add a new metric, e.g. average delivery time?** Add a new aggregate field to the Flink job and a new bucket column/family; the ingestion, partitioning, and serving layers are unchanged. That's the benefit of the bucket abstraction. > 💬 "New metric = new aggregate in the same pipeline; the plumbing doesn't change." **12. Thousands more concurrent viewers — what scales?** The read tier: add API replicas and Redis read replicas/shards, and lean harder on the CDN TTL since dashboards poll identical data. Ingestion is unaffected because viewers don't touch the write path. > 💬 "Reads scale independently — more API and Redis replicas, longer edge caching." **13. How do you guarantee an owner sees their own just-placed order (read-your-own-writes)?** Within the 60s freshness bound the snapshot updates; for a stronger guarantee on the owner's own view, the API can read-through to Cassandra buckets for that restaurant which reflect the latest processed delta. We surface `as_of` so expectations are honest. > 💬 "Within a minute it's there; if they need instant, I read-through to buckets for their own restaurant." **14. PII / data retention?** Aggregates hold no customer PII — just restaurant-owned item/value counts. Raw events with any user identifiers live in the warehouse under a retention policy with right-to-erasure handled by keying deletes on order/user id. > 💬 "Metrics are PII-free aggregates; raw data has retention and erasure handled in the warehouse." 🎙️ **60-second verbal summary:** "We provide restaurant owners real-time totals and top-K items over a rolling hour, day, and week. Orders flow through Kafka partitioned by restaurant into Flink, which keeps running per-restaurant aggregates and updates them with a slide trick — add the new time slice, subtract the expired one — so windows are constant-cost, not rescans. Durable buckets land in Cassandra, and ready-to-serve snapshots plus top-K sorted sets sit in Redis, so a dashboard read is one in-memory lookup behind a CDN, easily under 200ms for thousands of users with sub-minute freshness. Writes are tiny, so I spend the budget on read serving and window maintenance, keep minute buckets only for the hour and hourly rollups for day and week to bound storage, and handle hot mega-chains with a dedicated shard and a SpaceSaving sketch. Everything's idempotent by order id with cancellations as negative deltas, and since the raw log is the source of truth, recovery is just replay — RPO near zero."

▸
View problem statement
Problem statement
Design a system for a food delivery platform (like Uber Eats) that provides restaurant owners with real-time aggregated metrics about their orders. The system should process a continuous stream of new order data and make the following metrics available on a dashboard:

Total Value of Orders: For each restaurant, the sum of all order values within the last 1 hour, last 1 day, and last 1 week.
Top K Food Items: For each restaurant, a list of the top K most ordered food items (by quantity) within the last 1 hour, last 1 day, and last 1 week.
The metrics should be as real-time as possible, reflecting changes almost immediately after an order is placed (e.g., sub-minute refresh rate for dashboards).

Consider aspects such as data ingestion, real-time processing, storage solutions for aggregated and raw data, and efficient retrieval for dashboards used by thousands of restaurant owners.

Requirements:
Data Ingestion: Handle a high volume of incoming order data from various restaurants (millions of orders per day).
Real-time Processing: Aggregate data in near real-time to update metrics for different time windows.
Historical Data: Store and manage historical data efficiently for these time-windowed aggregations.
Querying: Efficiently serve queries for aggregated metrics for individual restaurants, catering to thousands of concurrent users.
Scalability: The system must be able to scale horizontally to accommodate growth in orders, restaurants, and active users.
Reliability & Accuracy: Ensure data accuracy, consistency, and high availability of the metrics system.

## 1. Requirements (Functional + Non-Functional)

> 🎙️ "Here's how I'll structure this: I'll lock the requirements and rough scale, sketch the API and data model, draw the high-level design one metric at a time, then go deep on the two or three hardest parts — the windowed aggregation and the top-K — checking in with you as I go. Sound good?"

The core tension: we need **near-real-time** (sub-minute) metrics over **sliding time windows** (1h / 1d / 1w) for **millions of orders/day**, served to **thousands of concurrent dashboards** — so the whole game is pre-aggregating on the write path so reads are cheap, while keeping windows accurate as they slide.

🏢 I'll target a **Google/Meta-style bar** — real distributed-streaming depth — but this problem smells like Uber/DoorDash, so I'll keep real-time and hot-restaurant skew front-of-mind. Tell me if it's Amazon and I'll make cost/ops lead.

**Functional (above the line):**
- **FR1 — Total order value per restaurant** over last 1h, 1d, 1w (rolling).
- **FR2 — Top-K food items per restaurant** by quantity over last 1h, 1d, 1w.
- **FR3 — Serve these to a dashboard** with sub-minute freshness for thousands of concurrent owners.

**Below the line (out of scope):** order placement/payments itself, delivery tracking, ML demand forecasting, owner auth UI, arbitrary ad-hoc OLAP (we'll mention a cold path for it).

**Non-Functional:**
- **Freshness:** metric reflects an order within **< 60s** (p99 end-to-end ingest→visible).
- **Read latency:** dashboard query **p99 < 200ms**.
- **Availability:** **99.9%** for the read/dashboard path (metrics are important but not payment-critical); ingestion **99.99%** (never drop orders).
- **Accuracy:** totals within **~1%** of exact in steady state, **eventually exact**; counts must not double-count on retries (idempotent).
- **Scale:** millions of orders/day, horizontally scalable on orders, restaurants, and viewers.
- **Consistency:** metrics are **eventually consistent** with a bounded staleness of one window-slice (~60s); the *owner's own* recent orders should appear within that bound (read-your-own-writes within 60s).

> ⚠️ Gaps I'd flag out loud: a true *sliding* 1-week window that's exact-to-the-second is expensive; I'll approximate it with fixed buckets (minute/hour granularity) and be explicit about the error bound rather than pretend it's exact.

> 💬 "The whole problem is reads vastly outnumber meaningful writes and the windows slide, so I'll pre-aggregate into time buckets on ingest and make the dashboard a cheap lookup."

🎙️ **Script:** "So we're building owner-facing analytics: total order value and top-K items, each over a rolling hour, day, and week, refreshing in under a minute. We take millions of orders a day in and fan them out to thousands of dashboards, so it's read-heavy on serving but the real work is keeping sliding-window aggregates fresh and accurate. I'll treat ingestion as can't-drop-data, and the metrics themselves as eventually consistent within about a minute — and I'll be upfront that a to-the-second weekly window is something I'll approximate with buckets."

## 2. Clarifying Questions & Assumptions

Questions I'd ask, and why each changes the design:
- **"How many orders/day and peak multiplier?"** — sets stream partition count and whether a single aggregator node can hold hot-restaurant state. *Assume ~10M/day, 5x peak.*
- **"How many restaurants and how skewed?"** — top-K state is per-restaurant; a few mega-chains create hot keys. *Assume ~500K restaurants, heavy skew (Zipfian).*
- **"How big is K?"** — K≈10–100 changes whether exact counts or a sketch is fine. *Assume K ≤ 20.*
- **"Exact or approximate weekly numbers acceptable?"** — decides exact bucket-sum vs. sketches (Count-Min/SpaceSaving). *Assume ~1% error OK, eventually exact.*
- **"Can orders be cancelled/modified after placement?"** — needs negative deltas / retractions, not just additive counts. *Assume yes — cancellations happen; design for reversible aggregates.*
- **"Order value currency/timezone for windows?"** — window boundaries and currency normalization. *Assume UTC windows, value pre-normalized to USD cents.*
- **"Is late/out-of-order event data common?"** — drives watermarks and allowed-lateness. *Assume seconds-to-minutes lateness possible (mobile clients).*

🤝 Checkpoint: "Those are my assumptions — 10M orders/day, 500K skewed restaurants, K≤20, ~1% accuracy with eventual exactness, and cancellations exist. Want to adjust any before I size it?"

Let me now compute the few numbers that actually drive decisions.

## 3. Scale & Capacity (talkable numbers) I'm deferring full estimation and only computing what forces a decision. | Metric | Number | Say-it-out-loud | |---|---|---| | Avg ingest | 116 orders/s | "~100/s average" | | Peak ingest | ~580 orders/s | "~600/s at peak" | | Dashboard reads (10% owners, 30s poll) | ~1,700 QPS | "under 2K reads/s" | | Raw orders storage | ~3 GB/day, ~21 GB/week | "tiny — gigabytes" | | Per-minute value buckets (1wk) | ~5B rows, ~200GB | "big if I go 1-min for a week" | | Hot per-restaurant state in Redis | ~730 MB | "fits in one Redis node's RAM" | **The one number that forces the design:** ingest is only **~600 writes/s at peak** — trivially small — but reads are **~1,700 QPS** and the *aggregate-maintenance* cost is what hurts. The flip point: if I keep **1-minute buckets for a full week per restaurant** I get ~5B rows / ~200GB and huge fan-out; that's the threshold where I switch to **mixed granularity** (1-min buckets only for the hour window, roll up to hourly buckets for day/week). That single decision cuts storage ~20x. 🧠 **QPS** *(queries per second — how many requests/sec the tier must serve)*. **TTL** *(time-to-live — auto-expiry on a cache entry so stale data self-cleans)*. 🎙️ **Script:** "The surprise here is that writes are tiny — about 600 orders a second at peak — so ingestion isn't the bottleneck at all. The pressure is on keeping sliding-window aggregates fresh and serving roughly 1,700 dashboard reads a second. The trap is storage on the aggregates: if I naively keep one-minute buckets for a whole week per restaurant that's five billion rows. So I'll keep fine one-minute buckets only for the hour view and roll up to hourly buckets for the day and week — that keeps it small and fast." ## 4. Core Entities - **Order** — a placed order: id, restaurant, list of (item, qty, price), total value, event timestamp. - **Restaurant** — the owner-facing tenant; everything is partitioned by restaurant id. - **Food Item** — a menu item within a restaurant; the unit we rank for top-K. - **Time-Bucket Aggregate** — pre-computed sum / item-counts for one (restaurant, granularity, bucket) slice. This is the heart of the system. - **Metric Snapshot** — the materialized answer for one (restaurant, window) the dashboard reads. This is a first draft of the nouns — I'll nail the exact fields in the data model after the high-level design. 🎙️ **Script:** "The key nouns are Orders coming in, Restaurants as the tenant we partition everything by, Food Items as what we rank, and then the thing that actually makes this fast — pre-computed time-bucket aggregates that the dashboard just reads as a snapshot. I'll firm up fields later." ## 5. API / Interface Default REST. Ingestion is internal (from the order service via a stream), dashboards read. **FR1/FR2 reads — one endpoint, window as param:** ``` GET /v1/restaurants/{id}/metrics?window=1h|1d|1w Auth: owner JWT; {id} must match token's restaurant claim 200 -> { window: "1h", total_value_cents: 1234500, top_k_items: [{item_id, name, qty}, ...], as_of: "2025-09-01T12:34:00Z" // freshness stamp } ``` Why GET: pure read, cacheable, CDN/edge-friendly. `as_of` lets the UI show freshness and lets us detect staleness. **Ingestion (internal, not public REST) — events onto the log:** ``` OrderPlaced { order_id, restaurant_id, items:[{item_id, qty, unit_price_cents}], total_cents, ts } OrderCancelled { order_id, restaurant_id, ts } // emits negative deltas ``` Each event carries `order_id` as the **idempotency/natural key** so retries dedup. **Security:** caller identity comes from the **JWT** *(signed token proving who's logged in)* — never trust a client-supplied restaurant id, timestamp, or value. An owner can only read their own restaurant. > 💬 "One read endpoint parameterized by window keeps the API tiny; ingestion isn't a public API, it's events the order service already emits." 🎙️ **Script:** "On the read side it's literally one GET per restaurant with the window as a query param, returning the total, the top-K list, and an as-of timestamp so the UI can show how fresh it is. Writes aren't a public API — the order service emits OrderPlaced and OrderCancelled events keyed by order id, and that order id is my idempotency key so a retried event never double-counts." ## 6. High-Level Design (built one requirement at a time) ### 6.1 "Total order value per restaurant over 1h / 1d / 1w" Start minimal: an order event arrives, we add its value into time buckets for that restaurant, and the dashboard reads the sum of the relevant buckets. New components: an **event log** (Kafka), a **stream processor** (Flink), a **bucket store**, and a serving cache. 🧠 **Kafka** *(a durable, replayable log that buffers a firehose of events so nothing is lost if a consumer is slow — here it holds order events between the order service and our processors)*. 🧠 **Flink** *(a stream-processing engine that maintains running aggregates over time windows with exactly-once guarantees — it does the per-restaurant summing)*. ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph Clients OS[Order service] DB1[Owner dashboard] end subgraph Ingest K[[Kafka order log
partition by restaurant]] end subgraph Stream F[Flink aggregator
value sums] end subgraph Serving AGG[(Bucket store
restaurant minute sum)] RD[(Redis
window snapshots)] API[Metrics API] end OS -->|1 OrderPlaced| K K -->|2 consume| F F -->|3 upsert bucket delta| AGG F -->|4 update window snapshot| RD DB1 -->|5 GET metrics| API API -->|6 read snapshot| RD API -.->|6b miss recompute| AGG classDef hot fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3; classDef cold fill:#3d1f0b,stroke:#d29922,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3; class RD,API,DB1 hot; class K,F,AGG,OS cold; ``` Flow: **(1)** order service publishes OrderPlaced to Kafka partitioned by restaurant_id (so all of one restaurant's events land on one partition → one consumer → no cross-node locking). **(2)** Flink consumes. **(3)** It upserts the order value into the current **minute bucket** `(restaurant, minute) += value` (state change: bucket sum increases). **(4)** It refreshes the three window snapshots (1h = sum of last 60 minute-buckets, etc.) in Redis. **(5)** Dashboard GETs. **(6)** API returns the Redis snapshot; on miss it recomputes from the bucket store. > 💬 "Partition Kafka by restaurant so each restaurant's stream is owned by exactly one processor — that removes locking and makes the aggregate trivially correct per key." ### 6.2 "Top-K food items per restaurant over each window" Same pipeline, second aggregate. For each item in the order, Flink increments a per-restaurant, per-bucket **item-count** map; the top-K is the highest counts across the window's buckets. New component: an **item-count state** in Flink + a top-K materializer. For the hot path we keep a small **heap/sorted-set** per (restaurant, window) in Redis (a Redis Sorted Set gives ordered top-K natively). 🧠 **Redis Sorted Set** *(an in-memory structure that keeps members ranked by score — here item → quantity — so reading top-K is an O(log n) range read, no scan)*. ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph Clients OS[Order service] DB1[Owner dashboard] end K[[Kafka order log]] subgraph Stream F[Flink aggregator
value + item counts] end subgraph Serving AGG[(Bucket store
value + item counts
per minute/hour)] RD[(Redis
value snapshots +
topK sorted sets)] API[Metrics API] end OS -->|1 OrderPlaced| K K -->|2| F F -->|3 value + item deltas| AGG F -->|4 update snapshot + ZSET| RD DB1 -->|5 GET| API API -->|6 read snapshot + ZRANGE| RD classDef hot fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3; classDef cold fill:#3d1f0b,stroke:#d29922,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3; class RD,API,DB1 hot; class K,F,AGG,OS cold; ``` Flow adds: **(3)** Flink also writes per-item count deltas; **(4)** updates the per-window Sorted Set in Redis (`ZINCRBY restaurant:1h item_42 qty`); **(6)** dashboard reads top-K via `ZREVRANGE … 0 K`. > 💬 "Top-K is just a ranked set per restaurant per window — Redis sorted sets give me ordered top-K for free, so the read is a single range query." ### 6.3 "Serve thousands of dashboards with sub-minute freshness" The serving layer is **read-from-Redis** behind a stateless API tier, fronted by a load balancer. Snapshots are refreshed by Flink every ~10–30s, so a poll-every-30s dashboard always sees < 60s-old data. We add a short **CDN/edge TTL (5–10s)** on the GET to absorb refresh-spam from many tabs. 🧠 **CDN** *(edge cache near users — here it collapses many identical dashboard polls into one origin hit for a few seconds)*. **Final (high-level):** ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph Clients OS[Order service] DB1[Dashboards x1000s] end CDN[CDN / edge cache
5-10s TTL] LB[Load balancer] K[[Kafka
partition by restaurant]] subgraph Stream F[Flink aggregator
value sums + item counts
checkpointed state] end subgraph Serving RD[(Redis cluster
snapshots + topK ZSETs
key=restaurant:window)] API[Metrics API

Flink keeps running sums and item counts, writes durable rollup buckets to Cassandra, and pushes ready-to-serve snapshots and top-K sorted sets into Redis every ten to thirty seconds. The dashboard just does one GET that hits a short-lived CDN cache, then Redis — so a thousands-of-users read is a single in-memory lookup, and the data is always under a minute old. Raw events also get archived to a warehouse so I can replay and audit." 🤝 Checkpoint: "That's the skeleton end to end. The two hardest parts are the sliding-window aggregation as windows expire and the top-K under hot-restaurant skew — which do you want me to go deep on first?" ## 7. Data Model & Storage | Entity | Key fields | Store | Why | |---|---|---|---| | Raw order event | order_id (PK), restaurant_id, items[], total_cents, ts | **Kafka → S3/warehouse** | Append-only firehose; cheap, replayable, audit/recompute source of truth | | Value bucket | (restaurant_id, granularity) part, bucket_ts clust, sum_cents, order_count | **Cassandra** | Write-heavy upserts + range-scan a window; partition by restaurant scales horizontally | | Item-count bucket | (restaurant_id, granularity, bucket_ts) part, item_id clust, qty | **Cassandra** | Same access pattern; range-scan items in a bucket | | Window snapshot | restaurant:{id}:{window} → total + topK ZSET | **Redis cluster** | Sub-ms reads for the dashboard hot path; TTL self-heals | 🧠 **Cassandra** *(a horizontally-partitioned NoSQL store optimized for high write throughput and key-range reads via an LSM-tree — here it holds the durable time buckets, sharded by restaurant)*. **LSM-tree** *(write-optimized structure that batches writes in memory then flushes sorted files — great for our upsert-heavy buckets)*. **Consistency per op:** bucket upserts are **idempotent by order_id** (we store processed order_ids with TTL to dedup replays); snapshot reads are **eventually consistent** (≤ window-slice stale). Cancellations write **negative deltas** into the current bucket so totals self-correct. **Granularity decision (from §3 math):** minute buckets for the 1h window; hourly buckets for 1d/1w. This caps the week scan at 168 hourly buckets instead of 10,080 minute buckets. 🎙️ **Script:** "Raw events live cheaply in Kafka and a warehouse as the source of truth. The durable aggregates go in Cassandra, partitioned by restaurant and clustered by bucket time, because the access pattern is exactly 'upsert this bucket, then range-scan the last N buckets.' The dashboard never touches Cassandra on the happy path — it reads Redis. And I keep minute buckets only for the hour, hourly for the day and week, which is what keeps storage sane." ## 8. Deep Dives — Bad → Good → Great 🆘 If you get stuck: go back to "writes are tiny, reads and window-maintenance are the cost," and reason forward from the bucket model. ### How do we compute sliding 1h/1d/1w windows in real time? **#### Bad: recompute from raw orders on each dashboard read.** Approach: on GET, scan all of a restaurant's orders in the window and sum. Challenges: a popular restaurant has thousands of orders/week; 1,700 read QPS each scanning thousands of rows melts the DB. ⚠️ **Trap:** doing aggregation at read time — it can't hit p99 200ms and doesn't scale with viewers. **#### Good: tumbling-window aggregation in Flink, one row per minute.** Approach: Flink maintains a per-(restaurant, minute) tumbling window; on read, sum the last 60 minutes / 24 hours / 7 days of buckets. Challenges: a *strict* sliding window needs the oldest slice to drop continuously; summing 10,080 minute-buckets for a week per read is wasteful, and storage explodes (the 5B-row problem). **#### Great: hierarchical buckets + precomputed rolling snapshot.** Approach: keep **minute buckets** (for 1h) and **hourly rollups** (for 1d/1w). Flink keeps a *running snapshot* per window and updates it incrementally: each minute it **adds the new slice and subtracts the expired slice** — this is the sliding window done as O(1) deltas, not a re-scan. Snapshots live in Redis; Cassandra holds buckets for recovery/recompute. ```mermaid flowchart LR E[Order event] --> M[Minute bucket +value] M --> H[Hourly rollup] M -->|add new slice| S1[1h snapshot] S1 -->|subtract expired minute| S1 H -->|add/subtract hour| S2[1d snapshot] H --> S3[1w snapshot] S1 --> RD[(Redis)] S2 --> RD S3 --> RD classDef hot fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3; class S1,S2,S3,RD hot; ``` Math: week window = 168 hourly buckets, not 10,080 minute buckets → ~60x less work and storage. Why real systems do this: incremental add/subtract on fixed buckets gives true rolling windows cheaply, and the bucket store lets us **replay** to fix errors. 🎙️ **Script:** "I keep minute buckets for the hour view and roll up to hourly buckets for day and week. The trick for sliding windows is that I don't re-sum on read — Flink keeps a running snapshot and each minute it adds the new slice and subtracts the one that just expired, so it's constant work. Redis holds the ready snapshot, Cassandra holds the buckets so I can always recompute." 🧠 If they ask "why not exact-to-the-second windows?": "Exact second-granularity for a week is 600K slices per restaurant — the storage and per-read cost don't justify it. Minute granularity bounds error to under one minute of orders, well within my ~1% accuracy SLA." ### How do we keep Top-K under hot-restaurant skew? **#### Good: exact per-item counters + Redis Sorted Set per window.** Approach: `ZINCRBY restaurant:{id}:{window} qty item` on each order; read `ZREVRANGE 0 K`. For a typical restaurant with ~30–200 distinct items this is exact and tiny. Challenges: a mega-chain promotion can make one ZSET huge and one Redis shard hot; and item counts also need the expired-slice subtraction. **#### Great: bucketed item counts + SpaceSaving sketch for huge tenants.** Approach: For normal restaurants, exact bucketed counts (add new minute, subtract expired). For the rare **giant tenant**, use a **SpaceSaving / Count-Min sketch** *(a fixed-memory structure that tracks approximate top items without storing every key)* capped at a few hundred counters — top-K of K≤20 stays accurate even if the long tail is approximate. Shard hot restaurants across Redis with a per-restaurant key so one mega-tenant doesn't collocate with others. 🧠 **Count-Min Sketch** *(a compact array of counters hashed multiple ways that estimates item frequencies in fixed memory — used so a viral item's restaurant doesn't blow memory)*. ⚠️ **Trap:** assuming uniform restaurants — a celebrity-chain promotion is the hot-key problem here. Relief: dedicated shard + sketch; new ceiling is bounded memory regardless of cardinality. 🎙️ **Script:** "For top-K I lean on Redis sorted sets keyed per restaurant per window, so reading the top items is one ranged query. Most restaurants have a small menu so exact counts are cheap. For a rare mega-chain that could blow up one shard, I switch that tenant to a bounded SpaceSaving sketch — top-20 stays accurate while the long tail is approximate — and I give hot tenants their own shard so they don't poison neighbors." 🧠 If they ask "won't the sketch be inaccurate?": "Only for the long tail; SpaceSaving guarantees the true top items aren't missed when their frequency exceeds the error bound, which for K≤20 on a high-volume tenant always holds." ### How do we guarantee accuracy with retries, cancellations, and late events? **#### Great: idempotent processing + exactly-once Flink + retractions + watermarks.** Approach: Each event has `order_id`; Flink uses **checkpointing + idempotent Cassandra upserts keyed by order_id** so a replay after failure doesn't double-count (**exactly-once** effect). Cancellations emit **negative deltas** keyed to the original order so totals self-correct. **Watermarks + allowed-lateness** *(a moving "we've seen everything up to time T" marker)* let late mobile events land in the right bucket within a grace window; events later than the grace go to a correction job that recomputes from raw S3 nightly → **eventually exact**. 🎙️ **Script:** "Accuracy rests on three things: every event is keyed by order id so retries are idempotent and Flink's checkpointing gives me effectively exactly-once, cancellations are just negative deltas into the bucket, and watermarks let slightly-late orders still land correctly. Anything later than the grace window gets fixed by a nightly recompute from the raw archive, so the numbers converge to exact." 🧠 If they ask "exactly-once is hard — really?": "End-to-end exactly-once needs idempotent sinks, which I have via order_id upserts; Flink gives exactly-once on its state, the sink dedups, so the combination is effectively exactly-once without distributed transactions." ## 9. Reliability, Failure Modes & Cost **Availability:** read path **99.9%** (multi-AZ Redis + stateless API behind LB); ingestion **99.99%** (Kafka replication factor 3). Per-dependency degradation: - **Redis down:** API falls back to recompute from Cassandra buckets (slower, ~50ms) — degraded, not down. Serve `as_of` so UI shows staleness. - **Flink down:** Kafka buffers (retention 24h+); on recovery it replays from last checkpoint — no data loss, just freshness lag. Dashboards show last-known + staleness banner. - **Cassandra down:** snapshots still served from Redis; bucket writes queue in Flink state. - **Hot shard:** dedicated shard + sketch (§8). **RPO/RTO:** Raw events in Kafka/S3 → **RPO ~0** (replicated log is source of truth). Aggregates are derived → **RTO minutes** by replay. Snapshots are disposable. Restore is tested by periodic replay-from-S3 into a staging cluster. **Multi-AZ vs region:** multi-AZ default; multi-region active-passive for DR (Kafka mirror), failover triggered by region health-check breaching for >2 min. **Cost (rough monthly):** dominant driver is **Kafka + Flink compute and Redis RAM**, not storage (raw is only ~21GB/week). Ballpark: a small Flink cluster + a ~3-node Redis cluster + Cassandra ~3 nodes → low **tens of thousands/month**. Egress is small (JSON metrics). **Ops:** SLO burn-rate alerts on freshness (as_of age > 90s) and read p99; distributed tracing ingest→Redis; runbook for "freshness lag" = check Flink checkpoint/lag. 🎙️ **Script:** "Because the raw event log is the source of truth and everything downstream is derived, my recovery story is just replay — RPO near zero, RTO in minutes. If Redis dies I recompute from Cassandra; if Flink dies Kafka buffers and we replay from a checkpoint; the dashboard always shows an as-of timestamp so degraded freshness is visible, not silent. Cost is dominated by streaming compute and Redis memory, not storage, since the raw data is only gigabytes." ## 10. Trade-off Ledger - **Minute/hourly bucket granularity vs exact sliding windows** — gave up to-the-second precision for ~60x less storage/compute; reverses if a customer demands second-level accuracy or order volume per restaurant drops enough that minute buckets for a week become cheap. - **Eventual consistency (≤60s) vs strong** — gave up instant exactness for cheap, scalable reads; reverses if metrics ever drive money (payouts) and need transactional accuracy — then read-from-buckets with stronger guarantees. - **Exact counts vs sketches for top-K** — exact by default, sketch only for mega-tenants; reverses fully toward sketches if restaurant menu cardinality grows huge or memory becomes the binding constraint. 🎙️ **Script:** "The three calls I'd flag: bucket granularity over exact windows, eventual consistency over strong, and exact counts over sketches except for giants. All three trade a little precision for a lot of scale, and each flips back if accuracy becomes worth more than cost — for instance if these numbers ever drove restaurant payouts." ## 11. Likely Interviewer Questions & Answers **1. Why pre-aggregate instead of a real-time OLAP DB like Druid/Pinot?** Druid/Pinot would actually fit this well and I'd happily use a managed one. I chose explicit Flink+buckets to control the sliding-window semantics and the hot-key/sketch handling precisely, and because raw volume is small. The trade-off is more components to operate vs. a single query engine. > 💬 "Honestly Pinot is a strong alternative here; I went custom for fine control of windows and hot keys, but I'd evaluate Pinot to cut operational surface." **2. How exactly do you expire the oldest slice from a rolling window?** Each window snapshot is `sum of buckets in [now-W, now]`. A timer fires per minute/hour; it adds the newest bucket and subtracts the bucket that just fell out of range. Buckets persist in Cassandra so a missed timer is recoverable by recompute. > 💬 "It's add-the-new-slice, subtract-the-expired-slice — constant work, not a rescan." **3. A celebrity restaurant gets 100x traffic — what breaks and what do you do?** Its Kafka partition and Redis shard become hot. Kafka stays fine (600/s total is low), but its ZSET and Flink task key get heavy. I give that tenant a dedicated Redis shard and switch its top-K to a bounded sketch, and I can sub-partition its Kafka key by item-hash if one task saturates. > 💬 "Isolate the hot tenant onto its own shard and cap its memory with a sketch." **4. How do you not double-count when Flink replays after a crash?** Cassandra upserts are keyed by order_id within a bucket, and Flink checkpoints its offsets; on replay it re-applies the same idempotent writes, so the result is identical. Snapshots are recomputed from buckets, so they're self-correcting. > 💬 "Idempotent upserts keyed by order id plus checkpointed offsets give effectively exactly-once." **5. An order is cancelled an hour later — does the total fix itself?** Yes — OrderCancelled emits a negative delta into the current bucket referencing the original order_id, so the running snapshot decreases. The nightly recompute from raw guarantees it's eventually exact even if the cancel arrived after the original bucket expired. > 💬 "Cancellations are negative deltas; the nightly recompute backstops anything that arrived late." **6. How is read p99 < 200ms achieved for 1,700 QPS?** Every happy-path read is a single Redis lookup plus a ZRANGE — sub-millisecond — behind a stateless API and a 5–10s CDN cache that collapses duplicate polls. Cassandra is only touched on Redis miss. > 💬 "It's one in-memory read; the DB isn't in the hot path." **7. What if Kafka is down?** The order service buffers/retries to a local outbox; metrics freshness lags but no orders are lost since order placement is a separate durable system. We alert on consumer lag and freshness. > 💬 "Kafka outage degrades freshness, not correctness — events replay when it's back." **8. Why partition by restaurant_id and not order_id?** All aggregates are per-restaurant, so co-locating a restaurant's events on one partition/task removes cross-node coordination and makes the windowed state local. Downside is skew, handled by sub-partitioning hot tenants. > 💬 "Per-restaurant partitioning makes the aggregate state local and lock-free." **9. How do you handle late/out-of-order events?** Flink watermarks with an allowed-lateness grace (a few minutes) route slightly-late events into the correct event-time bucket. Beyond the grace, they're caught by the nightly recompute from S3. > 💬 "Watermarks for the common lateness, nightly recompute for the stragglers." **10. Storage for a week of per-minute buckets is huge — how do you bound it?** I only keep minute granularity for the hour window and roll up to hourly buckets for day/week, cutting the week from ~10,080 slices to 168 per restaurant. Old minute buckets TTL out after the hour window passes. > 💬 "Mixed granularity plus TTL on fine buckets keeps storage at gigabytes." **11. How would you add a new metric, e.g. average delivery time?** Add a new aggregate field to the Flink job and a new bucket column/family; the ingestion, partitioning, and serving layers are unchanged. That's the benefit of the bucket abstraction. > 💬 "New metric = new aggregate in the same pipeline; the plumbing doesn't change." **12. Thousands more concurrent viewers — what scales?** The read tier: add API replicas and Redis read replicas/shards, and lean harder on the CDN TTL since dashboards poll identical data. Ingestion is unaffected because viewers don't touch the write path. > 💬 "Reads scale independently — more API and Redis replicas, longer edge caching." **13. How do you guarantee an owner sees their own just-placed order (read-your-own-writes)?** Within the 60s freshness bound the snapshot updates; for a stronger guarantee on the owner's own view, the API can read-through to Cassandra buckets for that restaurant which reflect the latest processed delta. We surface `as_of` so expectations are honest. > 💬 "Within a minute it's there; if they need instant, I read-through to buckets for their own restaurant." **14. PII / data retention?** Aggregates hold no customer PII — just restaurant-owned item/value counts. Raw events with any user identifiers live in the warehouse under a retention policy with right-to-erasure handled by keying deletes on order/user id. > 💬 "Metrics are PII-free aggregates; raw data has retention and erasure handled in the warehouse." 🎙️ **60-second verbal summary:** "We provide restaurant owners real-time totals and top-K items over a rolling hour, day, and week. Orders flow through Kafka partitioned by restaurant into Flink, which keeps running per-restaurant aggregates and updates them with a slide trick — add the new time slice, subtract the expired one — so windows are constant-cost, not rescans. Durable buckets land in Cassandra, and ready-to-serve snapshots plus top-K sorted sets sit in Redis, so a dashboard read is one in-memory lookup behind a CDN, easily under 200ms for thousands of users with sub-minute freshness. Writes are tiny, so I spend the budget on read serving and window maintenance, keep minute buckets only for the hour and hourly rollups for day and week to bound storage, and handle hot mega-chains with a dedicated shard and a SpaceSaving sketch. Everything's idempotent by order id with cancellations as negative deltas, and since the raw log is the source of truth, recovery is just replay — RPO near zero."
